title: Delete run directory after reboot
body:
### Issue Description

I am running podman on OEL9, and the issue is, when I reboot the host, can not start up container anymore. 
This folder will loose all contents:  /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers.
Error message: 
Error: unable to start container "xxxx": creating resolv.conf for container xxx: open /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/xxx/userdata/resolv.conf: no such file or directory

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Create and start container
2. Reboot server
3. Start container


### Describe the results you received

Error: unable to start container "xxxx": creating resolv.conf for container xxx: open /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/xxx/userdata/resolv.conf: no such file or directory
Then can see this folder was empty : /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers

### Describe the results you expected

Do not delete anything from this folder: /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers

### podman info output

comments.body:
Note we only support the latest podman upstream so please test with podman 5.5.2 or even better to 5.6-rc2 if possible.

`/run/containers` is the runroot and is expected to be wiped on reboot.

The per container runroot of /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/xxx is supposed to be recreated automatically post boot when the first podman command performs its refresh so you either have something miss configured or the refresh logic doesn't work correctly. The best case is to run `podman --log-level debug info` as fist command after theboot and see what it says.

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).

DEBU[0000] Called info.PersistentPostRunE(podman --log-level debug info)
DEBU[0000] Shutting down engines
INFO[0000] Received shutdown.Stop(), terminating!        PID=42577

Is this the podman inside our outside the container that fails?

From you output you did a podman ps before so the logs will not show refresh logic. It really must be the first podman command you run with --log-level debug. 

> Using tmp dir /run/libpod
> Using run root /run/containers/storage

Both of these paths must be empty on each reboot.

That output is from the host. Run after the reboot.
The if those folders are must be empty on each boot, what is generates this files again?
`[root@host zsolt]# podman ps
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE       COMMAND     CREATED     STATUS      PORTS       NAMES
[root@host zsolt]# podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                              COMMAND               CREATED        STATUS                         PORTS       NAMES
36d160497c98  localhost/container:24.10.9  /usr/bin/supervis...  3 minutes ago  Exited (0) About a minute ago              container
[root@host zsolt]# podman start container
Error: unable to start container "36d160497c98d4c444dccf39fd834611987f0b1bc3340823a5f848366db0d361": creating resolv.conf for container 36d160497c98d4c444dccf39fd834611987f0b1bc3340823a5f848366db0d361: open /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/36d160497c98d4c444dccf39fd834611987f0b1bc3340823a5f848366db0d361/userdata/resolv.conf: no such file or directory`

I am able to start container if I copy this dir back after the reboot: 36d160497c98d4c444dccf39fd834611987f0b1bc3340823a5f848366db0d361

[root@host zsolt]# cp -r 36d160497c98d4c444dccf39fd834611987f0b1bc3340823a5f848366db0d361 /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/
[root@host zsolt]# podman start container
container

I would have some questions.
1) If tmp dir and runroot dir must be empty after the reboot which process is create folders / files into /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/ ?
2) Where is stored the containers configs ?
3) is there any command line which generates files into /run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/ ?
4) is there any document which describe how to podman does before shutdown / after start up?

1. The `/run/containers/storage` directory is one of Podman's temporary files directories. Most Podman commands will interact with these directories. It is expected that the folders will be placed on tmpfs so they will be automatically removed on reboot; this is a critical part of how Podman detects reboots, and is necessary to properly clear the state and handle reboot.
2. Container configurations are stored in the `static dir` and `graph root` in the output of `podman info --debug`. There are several locations within those two where different bits of configuration live.
3. Almost any Podman command should do this. The problem you are seeing is that Podman cannot detect that your system has rebooted, and so does not know it needs to recreate the files. The problem of being unable to detect the reboot must be resolved by having the temporary directories cleared on reboot; there is no command that can be run after reboot to automatically resolve this problem, and I don't think we're going to add one. A hypothetical `podman system refresh` command would wreak absolute havoc on a system that had not gone through a reboot - you'd end up with orphan containers you couldn't stop, for example.
4. Not to the best of my knowledge, though the manpages do document our requirement that `tmp dir` and `runroot` should be on tmpfs - and, if that fails, should be automatically removed on reboot. AFAIK, we're installing systemd-tmpfiles scripts to cover the default locations for those, just in case they're not tmpfs.

All this being said, I'm rather surprised you're not seeing warnings displayed. Podman since 5.1.0 should be detecting unhandled reboots (via writing current systemd boot ID to disk in the temporary files directory, we should be able to identify an unhandled reboot), but I see no such warnings on your system.

> All this being said, I'm rather surprised you're not seeing warnings displayed. Podman since 5.1.0 should be detecting unhandled reboots (via writing current systemd boot ID to disk in the temporary files directory, we should be able to identify an unhandled reboot), but I see no such warnings on your system.

There is no warning because the dirs are deleted successfully otherwise there would be no need to re-create them. In fact systemd requires /run to be tmpfs so I am very sure it is successfully deleted.

So I think the real problem is that for some reason/refresh is not running or not working. That is why it is critical that you run the first command with `--log-level debug` because only the first command will do the refresh. We must see a log line like this
`Podman detected system restart - performing state refresh`

If you check podman events it should also log the refresh event.
```
# podman events --since 10m --stream=false
2025-08-19 05:57:00.577973941 -0400 EDT system refresh
```

If they are not there refresh is not being done and we must know why, manually creating the directory or trying to preserve it is not the answer.

Do you have any other podman commands via systemd or something else running that might run as first command and failing the refresh instead of your cli command?

Hi,
When I disabled podman systemd on the host was able to see log entry was you mentioned.

`INFO[0000] podman filtering at log level debug

But something is changed. I rebuild my test box which was build on OEL8 and OEL9.5, but now our image process is updated to OEL9.6 and looks not deleted the created containers.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I had this exact issue.  Although this was for user created pods, not system level podman pods.  The solution was to do a couple of steps so that data was not stored on a tmpfs file system.  It was quite maddening as if I tried to run ansible automation platform, and did a reboot command, nothing would come back up.

1.  Create ~/.config/containers/storage.conf

and put this in it:

[storage]

driver = "overlay"
runroot = "yourhomedirectory/run/"

2. podman sytem reset
3. podman info | grep -i runroot
    Make sure runRoot variable is set to "yourhomedirectory/run" or whatever directory you set it to that is not on a tmpfs filesystem.
4. re-create pods

runroot is supposed to be a tmpfs, chaining that to persistent disk like that is not recommend and will cause other problems like said above

podman's state refresh on first command is supposed to recreate the directories there.

So please provide proper information on what is happing on your systems and test with the latest podman.
you coul try to attach an debugger to the first command you run after boot and see if the refresh logic trigger and if ContainerRunDirectory is being called correctly.

You are probably right, but what I think is happening in my case is that these pods are badly written.  Although why ansible automation platform is written like this is kind of a mystery to me.  Or maybe I'm overlooking some sort of documentation on their site.
Parts of the postgresql pod that relies on resolv.conf, hostname, and hosts are put into transient locations.  So when a reboot is done, all this goes away, and the pod can't boot, complaining that these files are missing:

# podman inspect postgresql | grep run

          "ResolvConfPath": "/run/user/513264/containers/overlay-containers/a0652f1075a99589fb5ad5f67ca39a7f0117ce157eb82147f563d5a75003a1d1/userdata/resolv.conf",
          "HostnamePath": "/run/user/513264/containers/overlay-containers/a0652f1075a99589fb5ad5f67ca39a7f0117ce157eb82147f563d5a75003a1d1/userdata/hostname",
          "HostsPath": "/run/user/513264/containers/overlay-containers/a0652f1075a99589fb5ad5f67ca39a7f0117ce157eb82147f563d5a75003a1d1/userdata/hosts",
          "OCIRuntime": "crun",
          "ConmonPidFile": "/run/user/513264/containers/overlay-containers/a0652f1075a99589fb5ad5f67ca39a7f0117ce157eb82147f563d5a75003a1d1/userdata/conmon.pid",
          "PidFile": "/run/user/513264/containers/overlay-containers/a0652f1075a99589fb5ad5f67ca39a7f0117ce157eb82147f563d5a75003a1d1/userdata/pidfile",


,

No that has nothing to do with it, the files are supposed to be deleted on each boot, each container start will recreate these files just fine. In fact the create resolv.conf is what is failing because the directory  below does not exists for some reason, as pointed out here already the directory is created by or state refresh logic which runs in the first podman command after boot.

So the only question here is why does the state refresh not work for you, or if that works why is the directory deleted after state refresh but before the container start?
Unless you can answer these questions there is nothing we can help with.

ok, it is not ansible issue.  I have another identical system that is also running a similar configuration and the /run/user/*/containers/overlay-containers/* does get regenerated on reboot.  
The debug level commands on both systems seem to produce similar results on the system that is working and the system that is experiencing issues.  So I think I will have to just do a bit more investigating.  I will post if I find anything.

I was able to fix the issue.  Although how/why it was happening is still a mystery.  I had to compare the system that was working with the system that wasn't working, and the only difference was that podman.service was running on the one that was not working in both root and in the non-root user.   The only theory I can come up was that the podman.service that was enabled for user was interfering with the podman.service that was running as root.

# systemctl stop podman.service
# systemctl disable podman.service

Then log in as user:

$ systemctl --user stop podman.service
$ systemctl --user disable podman.service

Then reboot.
